top_risk: Per-user data isolation failure — a broken authz check on the stats endpoint leaks one user's XP/coins/rank to another
cheapest_evidence: Write the abuse-case walkthrough plus 2 authz tests (own-data 200, other-user-id 403) against the new read-only API before building any UI
report:
## (1) What is this really / core claim
A Phase-3 addition to the SuperBot site: a per-server 'personal stats' page where a Discord user logs in via Discord OAuth and sees their own XP, coins, mining details, and per-server leaderboard rank. Architecturally the claim is that the stats page must NOT touch the bot's production Postgres directly; instead a separate 'superbot-lane', read-only, token-scoped API service serving a versioned data contract mediates access. The compound claim to test: this phase is (a) buildable as designed and (b) trustworthy under adversarial verification, where the owner has set trustworthiness as an explicit public-launch gate covering six items: scope minimalism (identify + guilds only), session/token lifecycle with no long-lived token storage, CSRF/state correctness, per-user data isolation, rate limiting, and a documented abuse-case walkthrough. Phases 1 and 2 have no auth surface and are explicitly not gated on this.

## (2) Strongest failure mode
Per-user data isolation break. A single logic error converts the feature from 'trustworthy' to 'privacy incident': OAuth authenticates who the user is, but the stats query must independently enforce that returned rows belong only to that authenticated Discord ID for that guild. If the endpoint trusts a client-supplied user_id or guild_id, or an IDOR slips through, users read others' stats. Secondary: (a) the versioned read-only API is not actually isolated from production Postgres (shared credentials re-introduce the coupling the design forbids); (b) token-lifecycle drift where 'no long-lived token storage' is asserted but a session/refresh token quietly persists.

## (3) What would falsify it / cheapest adequate evidence
Falsifier: authenticated user A retrieves user B's XP/coins/rank. Buildability falsified if the read-only API cannot serve the contract without querying production Postgres live. Cheapest evidence: (i) the documented abuse-case walkthrough (required deliverable, costs only writing); (ii) two authz integration tests (own data 200, another Discord ID 403/empty); (iii) config check proving read-only, separately-scoped credentials. Scope minimalism verifiable by inspecting the app registration. Isolation guarantees beyond these tests: not measured.

## (4) Cost/complexity vs payoff
Cost dominated by the new standalone API service, not the page. Weight is building/operating a separate superbot-lane service with its own auth, token lifecycle, rate limiting, versioned contract, and read-only data path decoupled from production Postgres, plus the six-item gate. Payoff: a self-service personal-stats view per server; whether demand justifies a whole new service is not measured. Design is deliberately de-risked: DB-decoupling and gating are cost drivers that also ARE the mitigations; Phases 1-2 ship value with zero auth surface, so the risky phase is correctly isolated and last.

## (5) Smallest shippable slice
Ship Phases 1 and 2 first (no auth surface, explicitly ungated). For Phase 3: read-only token-scoped API serving ONE field (own XP for one guild) behind Discord OAuth identify+guilds, state-parameter CSRF, no persisted long-lived token, with the two isolation tests + written abuse-case walkthrough passing. Prove the isolation + no-prod-DB-coupling spine on one field before expanding. Exact effort estimates: not measured.
